Transnational Repression: the To-Read List Even as the United States stops promoting or even talking about democracy, the autocratic world continues not only to advertise and promote dictatorship, but to break the laws of the democratic world in pursuit of their dissidents. I wrote about this phenomenon back in 2021, after the Belarusian dictator forced a Ryanair flight out of the sky in order to arrest an activist on it : This is a story that belongs alongside the Russian use of radioactive poisons and nerve agents against enemies of the Kremlin in London and Salisbury, England ; Saudi Arabia’s brutal murder of one of its citizens inside a consulate in Istanbul; Iranian assassinations of dissidents in the Netherlands and Turkey; and Beijing’s kidnapping and detention of Chinese nationals living abroad and foreign citizens of Chinese origin. The human-rights organization Freedom House calls these new practices “transnational repression,” and has compiled more than 600 examples . All of these cases form part of what is becoming a new norm: Authoritarian states in pursuit of their enemies no longer feel the need to respect passports, borders, diplomatic customs, or—now—the rules of air-traffic control. This week, the Kremlin announced it was launching terrorist investigations of more than 20 Russian anti-war activists, all of whom live outside the country. Nataliya Vasilyeva of The New York Times notes that all of them belong to the Russian Antiwar Committee—a group of lawyers, law professors and exiled politicians led by Mikhail B. Khodorkovsky, a former Russian oligarch turned outspoken Kremlin critic. The investigation is the latest example of Russian state power—which has often included poisonings and shootings—being deployed against perceived enemies abroad. Read more here (gift link). China, meanwhile, has established shadow law enforcement agencies to harass its citizens the world, report Michael Forsythe, Jay Root, Bianca Pallaro, and David A. Fahrenthold, also for The New York Times. In 2023, for example, the FBI arrested the operators of a covert Chinese police station in New York, which tried to influence the behavior of citizens abroad by threatening their family members back home. Chinese consulate officials have also enlisted members of Chinese Communist Party–backed social clubs to harass and intimidate local politicians in New York who support Taiwan or are otherwise critical of the People’s Republic of China. China’s global influence apparatus has also harassed exiles in France, bribed academics in Britain, and targeted politicians in Canada for decades. Read more here (gift link). Smaller illiberal states are also capable of reaching beyond their borders. Rwanda’s longtime leader, Paul Kagame, has targeted political enemies in exile as well. In his review of Do Not Disturb by Michela Wrong, Phil Clark writes about the 2014 assassination of exiled Rwandan intelligence chief Patrick Karegeya, who had founded an opposition political party. Read more here (gift link). To sum up, Nate Schenkkan argues in the Journal of Democracy that we are living in a golden age of transnational repression , thanks to the decline of international law and the rise of sophisticated information and surveillance technologies. As autocracies around the world become more networked and coordinated, their ability to reach dissidents beyond their borders will only continue to grow. Read more here .

title: The Golden Age of Transnational Repression
sentence: Trumppardoned Binance founder Changpeng Zhao, who was convicted of violating U.S. anti–money laundering laws, after the crypto firm heavily promoted the World Liberty Financial stablecoin—paving the way for Binance to resume operations in the United States.Transnational Repression: the To-Read ListEven as the United States stops promoting or even talking about democracy, the autocratic world continues not only to advertise and promote dictatorship, but to break the laws of the democratic world in pursuit of their dissidents.I wrote about this phenomenon back in 2021, after the Belarusian dictator forced a Ryanair flight out of the sky in order to arrest an activist on it:This is a story that belongs alongside the Russian use of radioactive poisons and nerve agents against enemies of the Kremlin inLondonandSalisbury, England; Saudi Arabia’s brutalmurderof one of its citizens inside a consulate in Istanbul; Iranianassassinations of dissidentsin the Netherlands and Turkey; and Beijing’skidnapping and detentionof Chinese nationals living abroad and foreign citizens of Chinese origin. The human-rights organization Freedom House calls these new practices “transnational repression,” and hascompiled more than 600 examples.All of these cases form part of what is becoming a new norm: Authoritarian states in pursuit of their enemies no longer feel the need to respect passports, borders, diplomatic customs, or—now—the rules of air-traffic control.This week, the Kremlin announced it was launching terrorist investigations of more than 20 Russian anti-war activists, all of whom live outside the country. Nataliya Vasilyeva ofThe New York Timesnotes that all of them belong to the Russian Antiwar Committee—a group of lawyers, law professors and exiled politicians led by Mikhail B. Khodorkovsky, a former Russian oligarch turned outspoken Kremlin critic. The investigation is the latest example of Russian state power—which has often included poisonings and shootings—being deployed against perceived enemies abroad. Read morehere(gift link).China, meanwhile, has established shadow law enforcement agencies to harass its citizens the world, report Michael Forsythe, Jay Root, Bianca Pallaro, and David A. Fahrenthold, also forThe New York Times.In 2023, for example, the FBI arrested the operators of a covert Chinese police station in New York, which tried to influence the behavior of citizens abroad by threatening their family members back home. Chinese consulate officials have also enlisted members of Chinese Communist Party–backed social clubs to harass and intimidate local politicians in New York who support Taiwan or are otherwise critical of the People’s Republic of China. China’s global influence apparatus has also harassed exiles in France, bribed academics in Britain, and targeted politicians in Canada for decades. Read morehere(gift link).Smaller illiberal states are also capable of reaching beyond their borders. Rwanda’s longtime leader, Paul Kagame, has targeted political enemies in exile as well. In his review ofDo Not Disturbby Michela Wrong, Phil Clark writes about the 2014 assassination of exiled Rwandan intelligence chief Patrick Karegeya, who had founded an opposition political party. Read morehere(gift link).To sum up, Nate Schenkkan argues in theJournal of Democracythat we are living in agolden age of transnational repression, thanks to the decline of international law and the rise of sophisticated information and surveillance technologies. As autocracies around the world become more networked and coordinated, their ability to reach dissidents beyond their borders will only continue to grow. Read morehere. Afternoon in Antwerp
